result.markdown:
--- title: "A File Descriptor Trap" date: '2025-12-18' categories: - Operating Systems - QEMU description: | Troubleshooting QEMU networking issues with file descriptors. format: html: code-fold: true code-block-border-left: "#31BAE9" layout-nrow: 1 mermaid: theme: neutral --- ## Context I wanted a "real-ish" flat local network for Kubernetes on macOS that behaves like a real LAN, to *obscurely* test `MetalLB`and `kube-vip` The standard minikube NAT setup flattens all of that. So, I used: ```{sh} minikube start -p minikube \ --driver=qemu \ --network=socket_vmnet \ --nodes=2 \ --cni=cilium ``` I recently started using [lima](https://github.com/lima-vm/lima) to manage VMs in my workstation, along with [socket_vmnet](https://github.com/lima-vm/socket_vmnet) for networking, when using qemu vm_type. But, was getting: ```sh qemu-system-aarch64: -netdev socket,id=net0,fd=3: can't get socket option SO_TYPE ``` So, I tried to understand what was going on with the file descriptors (FDs) and how they are inherited across processes, especially in the context of `socket_vmnet` and QEMU. ## How the kernel thinks about FDs ### File descriptors Every process has a file descriptor table, where each slot is an index into an open resource, such as a file, pipe, socket, or device (but, actually everything is a file in Unix). That integer index is the file descriptor (FD), where $i \in W$. By convention, the kernel pre-populates three slots for every process: | FD | Name | Connected to | |----|--------|--------------| | 0 | stdin | keyboard (or pipe input) | | 1 | stdout | terminal (or pipe output) | | 2 | stderr | terminal (error output) | ```{mermaid} flowchart LR subgraph table["Process — FD Table"] direction TB fd0["fd 0 — stdin"] fd1["fd 1 — stdout"] fd2["fd 2 — stderr"] fd3["fd 3 — next open file / pipe / socket…"] end fd0 --> stdin["keyboard"] fd1 --> stdout["terminal"] fd2 --> stderr["terminal"] fd3 --> unknown["open file description
(file / pipe / socket / device)"] ``` ```{mermaid} graph LR subgraph clients [Client Layer] A([Web App]) --> B[API Gateway] C([Mobile App]) --> B end subgraph services [Service Layer] B --> D[Auth Service] B --> E[User Service] B --> F[Order Service] end subgraph data [Data Layer] D --> G[(Auth DB)] E --> H[(User DB)] F --> I[(Order DB)] F --> J([Message Queue]) end ``` Each slot points to an **open file description** — a kernel abstraction holding the offset, access mode, and a pointer to the underlying resource. The FD number is just the index; the type is determined by what the description wraps. ### Why child processes inherit file descriptors When a process calls `fork()`, the child gets a copy of the parent's FD table, with each entry pointing to the same underlying descriptions. This is how Unix pipelines work. Take `ls | grep foo | sort` — three processes, communicating through pipes they never explicitly opened themselves: ```{mermaid} flowchart LR shell["shell
forks 3 children
opens 2 pipes"] subgraph pipe1["pipe 1"] p1w["write end → fd=1"] p1r["read end → fd=0"] end subgraph pipe2["pipe 2"] p2w["write end → fd=1"] p2r["read end → fd=0"] end ls["ls
fd=1 → pipe1 write"] grep["grep foo
fd=0 → pipe1 read
fd=1 → pipe2 write"] sort["sort
fd=0 → pipe2 read
fd=1 → terminal"] shell -->|"fork/exec"| ls shell -->|"fork/exec"| grep shell -->|"fork/exec"| sort ls --> p1w p1r --> grep grep --> p2w p2r --> sort ``` The shell opens the pipes, forks, and each child inherits the FD table. `ls` writes to stdout (fd=1) — which is now the pipe, not the terminal. `grep` reads from stdin (fd=0) — the other end of the same pipe. Neither process knows or cares; they just use fd=0 and fd=1 as always. When `fork()` is followed by `exec()`, the FD table carries over into the new binary — unless individual FDs were marked `FD_CLOEXEC` (close-on-exec), which tells the kernel to close them silently on exec. Without that flag, every open FD in the parent lives on in the child, even after it has become a completely different program. ### The contract in this integration `socket_vmnet` opens the socket and passes it to QEMU by FD number: ```text -netdev socket,id=net0,fd=3 ``` The convention: *"I've left the socket on fd=3 — use it."* During init, QEMU calls `getsockopt(fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_TYPE, ...)` to verify the FD is actually a socket. If not, the kernel returns `ENOTSOCK`, QEMU logs the error, and exits. ## What actually happened My terminal was **Hyper** — an Electron-based app. Electron opens extra FDs for its own IPC and logging, and does not mark them `FD_CLOEXEC`. One of them was FD 3, pointing to a regular data file: ```bash lsof -p $$ | awk '$4 ~ /^3[urw]/ {print}' ``` ```text zsh 581 ioaiaaii 3r REG ... /Applications/Hyper.app/.../icudtl.dat ``` `icudtl.dat` is Electron's ICU (Unicode data) file. It claimed fd=3 at startup and leaked it down the entire process chain: ```{mermaid} flowchart TD hyper["Hyper — Electron
opens icudtl.dat → fd=3
❌ no FD_CLOEXEC"] zsh["zsh
inherits fd=3 → icudtl.dat"] minikube["minikube
inherits fd=3 → icudtl.dat"] vmnet["socket_vmnet_client
opens UNIX socket → fd=5
⚠️ fd=3 already occupied"] qemu["QEMU
inherits fd=3 → icudtl.dat 📄
inherits fd=5 → UNIX socket 🔌"] crash["💥 CRASH
getsockopt(3, SOL_SOCKET, SO_TYPE)
kernel: ENOTSOCK"] hyper -->|"fork/exec"| zsh zsh -->|"fork/exec"| minikube minikube -->|"fork/exec"| vmnet vmnet -->|"fork/exec"| qemu qemu --> crash style hyper fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#e6ac00 style vmnet fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#e6ac00 style crash fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#c0392b ``` The socket was real — it just landed on fd=5 because fd=3 was already taken. QEMU was told to use fd=3 and never looked at fd=5. ### Confirming with `socket_vmnet_client` ```bash # Is fd=3 a socket? /opt/socket_vmnet/bin/socket_vmnet_client /var/run/socket_vmnet \ /bin/sh -lc 'python3 -c "import os,stat; print(stat.S_ISSOCK(os.fstat(3).st_mode))"' ``` ```text False ``` ```bash # Is fd=5 a socket? /opt/socket_vmnet/bin/socket_vmnet_client /var/run/socket_vmnet \ /bin/sh -lc 'python3 -c "import os,stat; print(stat.S_ISSOCK(os.fstat(5).st_mode))"' ``` ```text True ``` ## The fix Close fd=3 in the current shell before running minikube: ```bash exec 3>&- ``` `exec 3>&-` closes fd=3 without spawning a subshell. The kernel then assigns fd=3 to the next opened file — which will be the socket. To avoid any side-effects on your session, use a subshell: ```bash (exec 3>&-; minikube start -p minikube --driver=qemu --network=socket_vmnet --nodes=2) ``` ## Why this is a fragile design The root issue is that the integration **assumes a specific FD number is free** without enforcing it. Two robust patterns exist: **Pattern 1 — `dup2()` to force the socket onto fd=3:** ```c int sock = connect_to_vmnet_socket(); dup2(sock, 3); // atomically closes fd=3 if open, moves socket there close(sock); execvp("qemu-system-aarch64", args); ``` `dup2(oldfd, newfd)` duplicates `oldfd` onto `newfd`, closing `newfd` first if necessary. QEMU gets fd=3 as a socket regardless of what the parent had open. **Pattern 2 — close inherited FDs before exec:** Iterate over all open FDs and close anything that shouldn't be inherited. Standard practice in security-sensitive launchers. Electron terminals leaving extra FDs open without `FD_CLOEXEC` is the norm. Any launcher that depends on a specific FD number being free is at the mercy of its caller's environment. ## Takeaway `fd=3: can't get socket option SO_TYPE` means fd=3 is open, but it is not a socket. The number is right; the object behind it is wrong. Whenever an integration passes a file descriptor by number between processes, it establishes a contract about the FD table at exec time — a contract that breaks silently unless enforced with `dup2()` or explicit cleanup.
